title: Cardiac Trauma
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium
authors: Alexandra R. Pipilas, Eric H. Awtry

items.subtopic: Cardiac Trauma | CARDIAC TRAUMA | BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Importance of ECG and Troponin in Blunt Cardiac Injury
items.content: In blunt cardiac injury (BCI), an electrocardiogram (ECG) should be obtained for all patients with chest trauma to detect arrhythmias, conduction disturbances, or infarction-like changes. Elevated troponin levels are more specific than CK-MB for identifying cardiac damage; a normal serial troponin level at 6–8 hours post-trauma essentially excludes BCI when combined with a normal ECG.
keyPoints: Obtain an ECG in all patients presenting with chest trauma to detect abnormalities.
Cardiac troponin levels are more specific for cardiac damage than CK-MB, which may be falsely elevated due to skeletal muscle injury.
A normal serial troponin level at 6–8 hours post-trauma essentially excludes BCI when combined with a normal ECG.
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · Cardiac Trauma | CARDIAC TRAUMA | BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Cardiac Trauma | CARDIAC TRAUMA | BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Role of Echocardiography in Diagnosing Blunt Cardiac Injury
items.content: Echocardiography is crucial for detecting structural and functional sequelae of BCI, such as regional wall motion abnormalities, pericardial effusion, valvular dysfunction, and ventricular or septal rupture. A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) should be performed in patients with suspected BCI, especially those with abnormal ECG, elevated troponin, or hemodynamic instability.
keyPoints: Echocardiography is the most useful modality for detecting structural and functional sequelae of BCI.
Perform a TTE in all patients with suspected BCI, particularly if there are abnormal ECG findings, elevated troponin levels, or hemodynamic instability.
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · Cardiac Trauma | CARDIAC TRAUMA | BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Cardiac Trauma | CARDIAC TRAUMA | BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Commotio Cordis and Takotsubo Syndrome
items.content: Commotio cordis, an electrical phenomenon often seen in adolescents during sports, can cause ventricular fibrillation without structural myocardial damage. It occurs when the chest is struck over the heart during a susceptible phase of repolarization. Survival depends on prompt defibrillation. Takotsubo syndrome, or apical ballooning syndrome, may be triggered by sudden emotional or physical trauma and involves transient catecholamine-mediated cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: Commotio cordis can cause ventricular fibrillation without structural damage, often during sports in adolescents; survival depends on prompt defibrillation.
Takotsubo syndrome is a transient cardiomyopathy triggered by sudden emotional or physical trauma.
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · Cardiac Trauma | CARDIAC TRAUMA | BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 2) | Blunt Cardiac Injury
items.title: Management of Hemodynamically Stable Patients with Blunt Cardiac Injury
items.content: In patients with blunt cardiac injury who are hemodynamically stable and have normal ECGs and serial troponin levels, hospital admission for cardiac issues is typically unnecessary due to their low risk.
keyPoints: Hemodynamically stable patients with normal ECG and troponin levels at low risk for BCI
No requirement for hospital admission unless arrhythmias develop
24–48 hours of telemetry monitoring recommended if there are abnormal ECG or elevated troponin but normal echocardiogram
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 2) | Blunt Cardiac Injury | PENETRATING CARDIAC INJURY

items.subtopic: PENETRATING CARDIAC INJURY
items.title: Prognosis and Management of Penetrating Cardiac Injury
items.content: Penetrating cardiac injuries often lead to rapid clinical deterioration. The prognosis depends on the injury mechanism, involved cardiac chambers, and patient stability at presentation.
keyPoints: Gunshot wounds have higher mortality (~80%) compared to knife wounds (up to 35% mortality)
Right ventricle is most frequently injured due to its anterior position
RV injuries may have a better prognosis than LV injuries; multichamber involvement worsens prognosis
90% mortality in hemodynamically collapsed patients, ~75% survival if stable enough for surgery
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 2) | Blunt Cardiac Injury | PENETRATING CARDIAC INJURY

items.subtopic: PENETRATING CARDIAC INJURY
items.title: Diagnosis and Follow-up of Occult Penetrating Cardiac Trauma
items.content: Even in hemodynamically stable patients with penetrating chest injuries, a high index of suspicion for cardiac injury is necessary. Echocardiography should be performed to detect pericardial effusion or hematoma.
keyPoints: Up to 20% of stable patients may have occult cardiac trauma
Echocardiography (TTE) recommended for all penetrating chest injuries
Follow-up examination weeks after injury is crucial for detecting late complications like new murmurs or heart failure
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · BLUNT CARDIAC INJURY (part 2) | Blunt Cardiac Injury | PENETRATING CARDIAC INJURY

items.subtopic: Penetrating Cardiac Injury
items.title: Immediate Surgical Intervention for Hemodynamically Unstable Penetrating Cardiac Injuries
items.content: Patients with penetrating cardiac injuries presenting with hemodynamic instability require urgent surgical intervention. This includes endotracheal intubation, establishing large-bore intravenous access for massive volume resuscitation, and immediate thoracotomy to perform pericardial drainage and repair of cardiac injuries.
keyPoints: Surgical emergency in the presence of hemodynamic instability
Immediate interventions: endotracheal intubation, large-bore IV access, thoracotomy for pericardial drainage and repair
Cross-clamping of descending aorta may be required to achieve hemodynamic stability
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · Penetrating Cardiac Injury

items.subtopic: Penetrating Cardiac Injury
items.title: Surgical Exploration in Hemodynamically Stable Patients with Pericardial Effusion
items.content: Even in hemodynamically stable patients, the presence of a pericardial effusion identified by echocardiography necessitates urgent surgical exploration to rule out occult cardiac perforation. While pericardiocentesis can be lifesaving in cases of tamponade, it is generally considered only a temporary measure until definitive surgery can be performed.
keyPoints: Urgent surgical exploration required for stable patients with echocardiographic evidence of pericardial effusion
Pericardiocentesis may be lifesaving in tamponade but is typically a temporizing measure
Potential development of constrictive pericarditis post-injury, possibly requiring decortication
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · Penetrating Cardiac Injury

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common mechanism for blunt cardiac injury (BCI)?
options: Motor vehicle accidents due to rapid deceleration
items.explanation: Blunt cardiac injuries are typically caused by nonpenetrating trauma such as motor vehicle accidents, falls, and assaults.

items.question: Which condition can blunt chest trauma trigger even without structural myocardial damage?
items.explanation: Blunt chest trauma can trigger ventricular fibrillation, known as commotio cordis, without structural myocardial damage.

items.subtopic: Penetrating Cardiac Injury
items.question: What is the immediate management step for a patient with penetrating cardiac injury and hemodynamic instability?
options: Endotracheal intubation, establishment of large-bore intravenous access, and immediate thoracotomy
items.explanation: Immediate management includes endotracheal intubation, large-bore IV access for resuscitation, and thoracotomy.
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · Penetrating Cardiac Injury

items.subtopic: Penetrating Cardiac Injury
items.question: In hemodynamically stable patients with a small pericardial effusion detected on echocardiography, what is the recommended course of action?
options: Urgent surgical exploration to evaluate for occult cardiac perforation
Immediate discharge with follow-up in 24 hours
items.explanation: Hemodynamically stable patients with even a small pericardial effusion require urgent surgical exploration.
items.reference: Cardiac Trauma · Penetrating Cardiac Injury

items.subtopic: Penetrating Cardiac Injury
items.question: What is the role of pericardiocentesis in the management of penetrating cardiac injury?
options: It provides definitive treatment for cardiac injuries.
It may be lifesaving but is usually a temporizing measure until surgical therapy can be performed.
It should be avoided as it increases the risk of infection.
It is contraindicated in all cases of penetrating cardiac injury.
items.explanation: Pericardiocentesis may be lifesaving but typically serves as a temporary measure before definitive surgical therapy.

items.statement: Sternal or rib fractures are proven indicators of blunt cardiac injury.
items.explanation: There is no proven association between sternal or rib fractures and the presence of BCI.

items.subtopic: Penetrating Cardiac Injury
items.statement: Cross-clamping of the descending aorta is never required in penetrating cardiac injuries.
items.explanation: Cross-clamping of the descending aorta may be necessary to preferentially perfuse the heart and brain until stability is achieved.

items.statement: Pericardial hemorrhage from penetrating cardiac injury can lead to pericardial constriction.
items.explanation: Survivors of penetrating cardiac injury may develop constriction due to pericardial hemorrhage.

items.assertion: Commotio cordis often occurs in adolescents during sporting events.
items.reason: It results from an impact to the chest wall overlying the heart during a susceptible phase of repolarization.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why commotio cordis occurs in adolescents during sporting events.

items.assertion: Myocardial rupture is a minor consequence of nonpenetrating cardiac injury.
items.reason: It can result in hemopericardium and tamponade, which are often fatal.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because myocardial rupture is the most serious consequence of nonpenetrating cardiac injury.
